questions.question: Which statement is correct about local variables in Java?
options: They always store full object copies
They store either primitive values or reference values for the current frame
They live permanently in the heap
They are shared across all threads
questions.explanation: A stack frame stores local slots. Object instances remain elsewhere; the slot only holds the reference needed to reach them.

questions.question: Why can two variables observe the same object mutation?
options: Because Java duplicates heap objects automatically
Because the JVM reuses primitive slots for all objects
Because both variables can point at the same heap object
Because method calls force all objects into static memory
questions.explanation: The shared mutation comes from shared reference identity, not from the variable names themselves.

questions.question: What confusion does the stack vs heap lesson try to remove first?
options: Why generics use angle brackets
Why thread pools need queues
Why changing one alias can affect another alias
Why streams are lazy
questions.explanation: The initial mental-model fix is separating variable slots from heap objects so aliasing surprises make sense.

questions.question: What usually lives on the heap?
options: Object instances and their fields
Only primitive ints
Only method parameters
Only class names
questions.explanation: Heap storage is where object instances live; stack frames only hold the data needed for the active call.

questions.question: Which interview answer is strongest when asked why first and second both changed?
options: Because Java passes objects by reference
Because the compiler cloned the object twice
Because both variables store references to the same heap object and the mutation happened on shared state
Because the stack frame forced the values to merge
questions.explanation: The precise explanation is shared object identity through references; saying 'pass by reference' is the common but sloppy shortcut.
